entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 04 - Namespaces, contexts and name prefixes
Baseline date and net due date mismatch in the Import Supplier Invoices app (F3041)
entry.content:
Dear SAP supportteam,

We are facing an issue in the Import Supplier Invoices app (F3041). Invoices imported via the template sometimes show a mismatch between the baseline date and the net due date even on working days that are not bank holidays. We understand and use the Japan feature that adjusts due dates when they fall on bank holidays; however, the shifts we observe occur on non-holiday dates.
In the imported invoice’s Payment tab, the field “Days 1 / In Percent” displays the value “1”. We would like to know:

From which logic or source this “1” is derived and populated during import.
Whether this value is tied to the payment terms definition or defaulted by the app.
Whether this value contributes to the mismatch between the baseline date and the net due 
date.


Our expectation is that the net due date equals the baseline date plus the days defined in 
the payment terms, unless a bank-holiday adjustment applies.

Please help identify why the due date is shifted on non-holiday dates in F3041 and clarify the origin and calculation logic of “

entry.title: Application Decisions for Sales Order Management - Part 3
entry.content:
Summary

In previous deliverables, we examined the use of Sales Order Management capabilities within SAP Customer Experience solutions, including SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, from both functional/business and technical/architectural perspectives. Those documents explored how customer-facing solutions can support sales engagement, opportunity management, mobility, and omnichannel customer interactions.

You can access these documents here:

Application Decisions for Sales Order Management in CX - Part 1

Application Decisions for Sales Order Management in CX - Part 2

In hybrid architectures, SAP S/4HANA typically remains the system of record for fulfillment and operational execution. While, SAP Customer Experience solutions may manage customer interactions and order capture activities. SAP S/4HANA often orchestrates fulfillment, logistics, billing, financial postings, and downstream operational processes.

This document focuses on SAP S/4HANA Sales as a strategic option for Sales Order Management, describing its capabilities, strengths, limitations, and architectural considerations. The objective is not to position one solution as universally superior, but rather to help architects evaluate the most appropriate target architecture based on operational priorities, customer engagement strategy, user experience expectations, process complexity, and long-term transformation objectives.

Most of the information included in this document is publicly available through SAP resources such as the SAP Help Portal. However, it has been consolidated and summarized here into a single reference document for the convenience of Enterprise Architects and transformation teams.

Architectural Perspective

In many SAP transformation programs, Sales Order Management is no longer evaluated as an isolated application capability, but rather as part of a broader architectural discussion regarding customer engagement, operational integration, process orchestration, and application landscape simplification.

As organizations modernize their application landscapes, Enterprise Architects frequently need to determine whether Sales Order Management should remain primarily ERP-centric within SAP S/4HANA or evolve toward a more customer-centric architecture leveraging SAP Sales Cloud solutions.

This discussion is especially relevant because the decision often impacts not only the sales organization, but also logistics, fulfillment, finance, integration architecture, master data governance, reporting, user experience, and long-term operational complexity.

Note: The purpose of this document is not to provide a comprehensive description of all SAP S/4HANA Sales capabilities or processes. Instead, this article focuses on the capabilities, architectural considerations, and functional areas that are most commonly evaluated when organizations compare or assess SAP S/4HANA Sales against SAP Customer Experience cloud solutions as part of a target architecture definition or transformation initiative.

Solution Options

Depending on the target architecture and business priorities, multiple SAP solutions can support Sales Order Management scenarios. The most common solution options include:

  • SAP S/4HANA Sales
  • SAP Sales Cloud Version 2
  • SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) – Sales
  • Hybrid architectures combining SAP Sales Cloud solutions with SAP S/4HANA

In hybrid scenarios, SAP Customer Experience solutions are typically responsible for customer engagement, sales execution, opportunity management, and customer-facing activities, while SAP S/4HANA remains responsible for fulfillment orchestration, pricing execution, billing, logistics integration, and financial processing.

As mentioned previously, the primary focus of this article is SAP S/4HANA Sales and its role within modern Sales Order Management architectures.

Characterization of the Solution 

SAP S/4HANA Sales

SAP S/4HANA Sales provides comprehensive Sales Order Management capabilities tightly integrated with operational and enterprise processes such as logistics, warehousing, fulfillment, transportation, billing, finance, and supply chain execution.

One of the most important characteristics of SAP S/4HANA Sales is its ability to support the complete order lifecycle within a unified ERP environment. This enables organizations to maintain transactional consistency while minimizing the need for extensive replication scenarios, synchronization mechanisms, or additional integration layers between customer-facing and operational systems.

SAP S/4HANA Sales supports end-to-end Sales Order Management processes spanning from quotation and order creation through delivery, billing, and revenue recognition. Presales activities can be managed using business documents such as sales inquiries and sales quotations, allowing organizations to support commercial negotiations and customer interactions before the creation of a formal sales order.

Once a customer accepts a quotation and submits a purchase order, the process can trigger the creation of a sales order within SAP S/4HANA. The sales order then becomes the central transactional object driving subsequent operational activities such as availability checks, delivery processing, fulfillment execution, transportation planning, shipping, billing, and financial postings.

This tight integration between commercial and operational processes is one of the primary reasons why many organizations continue to position SAP S/4HANA Sales as the main platform for Sales Order Management. This is particularly common in industries where order processing is highly transactional and deeply connected with supply chain execution, manufacturing processes, inventory management, or complex fulfillment operations.

From a functional perspective, SAP S/4HANA Sales provides mature enterprise-scale capabilities in areas such as complex pricing, advanced Available-to-Promise (ATP), variant configuration, delivery orchestration, credit management, billing integration, and document flow visibility. These capabilities are especially important for organizations operating sophisticated pricing models, configurable product scenarios, high-volume order environments, or strict fulfillment requirements.

Another major advantage of SAP S/4HANA Sales is the native integration with financial and fulfillment processes. By operating within a centralized ERP platform, organizations can reduce replication complexity, centralize master data governance, improve process consistency, and achieve end-to-end operational visibility across the complete order lifecycle.

Additionally, SAP S/4HANA Sales benefits from embedded analytics and enterprise-grade scalability, allowing organizations to support large transaction volumes while maintaining operational transparency and integrated reporting capabilities directly within the ERP environment.

Criteria to determine the most suitable target solution

1. ERP-Centric vs Customer-Centric Strategy

One of the most important architectural decisions during a Sales transformation initiative is determining whether the organization wants to prioritize operational efficiency or customer engagement capabilities as the primary driver for the target solution.

SAP S/4HANA Sales is often a strong fit for organizations where the ERP system already acts as the operational backbone of the enterprise and where sales processes are deeply integrated with fulfillment, logistics, finance, manufacturing, or supply chain execution. In these environments, sales orders are not only commercial documents but also operational triggers that drive downstream business processes. As a result, keeping Sales Order Management directly within SAP S/4HANA can simplify process orchestration, reduce system fragmentation, and improve end-to-end operational visibility.

Organizations that already have ERP-oriented users and operationally focused sales teams may also find SAP S/4HANA Sales to be a natural extension of their existing business processes.

More information available in the Order and Contract Management in the SAP Help Portal.

2. Need for a Single UI

A common requirement observed during transformation programs is the desire to provide sales representatives with a single user interface for managing customer interactions and sales activities. Many organizations view this as an opportunity to simplify the user experience, reduce context switching between systems, and minimize overall application landscape complexity.

In SAP-centric environments, this frequently leads to evaluating whether SAP S/4HANA itself can serve as the primary interface for Sales Order Management activities. By consolidating processes into a single platform, organizations may reduce integration dependencies, simplify master data synchronization, and centralize transactional processing within the ERP environment.

However, while this approach may simplify architecture and operational management, it can also introduce important tradeoffs that should be carefully evaluated. SAP S/4HANA Sales primarily focuses on transactional execution and operational integration rather than customer engagement and sales collaboration.

As a result, organizations may experience limitations related to modern CRM capabilities, mobile-first experiences, customer activity management, guided selling scenarios, or omnichannel customer engagement.

For Enterprise Architects, the challenge is often not simply deciding between one UI or multiple systems, but rather determining whether the architectural simplification gained by consolidating into SAP S/4HANA justifies the potential reduction in customer engagement capabilities.

3. Pricing Complexity

Pricing is frequently one of the most influential factors when defining the target architecture for Sales Order Management. In many industries, pricing logic extends far beyond simple discounts or price lists and instead involves highly sophisticated pricing procedures, condition techniques, rebates, surcharges, customer-specific agreements, contractual pricing, and real-time operational calculations.

SAP S/4HANA Sales has historically been recognized for its mature and highly flexible pricing engine, making it particularly suitable for organizations operating complex pricing models or margin-sensitive business processes. Industries such as manufacturing, wholesale distribution, chemicals, automotive, and industrial products often rely on advanced pricing capabilities tightly integrated with logistics, fulfillment, procurement, or financial processes.

Another important consideration is the requirement for real-time ERP pricing. In many operational environments, pricing calculations depend on live ERP data such as inventory availability, transportation costs, taxes, contractual conditions, or manufacturing constraints. Maintaining pricing execution directly within SAP S/4HANA can therefore simplify consistency, reduce integration dependencies, and avoid discrepancies between front-office and ERP pricing calculations.

For additional information, please refer to the Price Management section in the SAP Help Portal.

4. Integration Complexity

Integration complexity is often a major consideration when evaluating Sales Order Management architectures. Organizations attempting to simplify their application landscapes frequently assess whether maintaining Sales Order Management directly within SAP S/4HANA can reduce the number of systems, interfaces, replication mechanisms, and middleware dependencies required to support end-to-end sales processes.

A primarily SAP S/4HANA-centric architecture can reduce integration overhead by centralizing transactional processing, pricing logic, master data management, and operational execution within a single ERP platform. This may simplify integration monitoring, reduce synchronization challenges, and minimize the need for complex replication scenarios between front-office and back-office applications.

However, hybrid architectures combining SAP Sales Cloud with SAP S/4HANA may still provide important strategic advantages despite the additional integration complexity. These architectures often enable better user experiences, stronger customer engagement capabilities, more flexible sales processes, and improved sales productivity for customer-facing teams.

As a result, the architectural discussion should not focus solely on reducing integrations, but rather on balancing simplification objectives against the business value provided by specialized customer engagement capabilities.

Additional details about integrations within Sales Order Management are available at the following link, including:

  • Integration with External Buyer Systems
  • Integration with SAP Business Network
  • Integration with SAP Global Trade Services
  • Integration with SAP Self-Billing Cockpit
  • Integration with SAP Subscription Billing

5. AI and Intelligent Sales

Artificial Intelligence is becoming an increasingly important factor in Sales transformation initiatives, particularly as organizations look for ways to improve sales productivity, customer engagement, forecasting accuracy, and decision-making capabilities.

SAP S/4HANA tends to focus AI capabilities more toward operational intelligence and ERP-centric processes. Examples include predictive fulfillment scenarios, embedded operational analytics, exception handling, process automation, and ERP-integrated AI use cases designed to improve operational efficiency and transactional visibility across the sales process.

Additionally, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition incorporates Joule, SAP’s generative AI copilot, which provides dedicated use cases for Sales processes. Joule supports users with transactional and operational activities directly within the SAP S/4HANA environment, helping improve productivity and simplify user interactions across sales execution scenarios.

Examples of Joule-supported sales use cases include:

  • Sales order fulfillment monitoring
  • Sales document creation with references
  • Mass changes to sales orders
  • Billing document creation with references
  • Renewing expiring prices
  • Sales quotation updates
  • Displaying lists of open sales documents

In this context, AI within SAP S/4HANA is typically positioned to enhance operational execution, transactional productivity, and enterprise process intelligence, while SAP Sales Cloud solutions remain more focused on customer engagement and sales relationship management scenarios.

For more information about this topic please refer to the Sales section in Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition of the SAP Help Portal site.

6. Offline Requirements

Offline capability remains an important consideration for organizations with mobile sales teams operating in environments where internet connectivity may be inconsistent or unavailable. This requirement is especially common in industries such as consumer products, field sales, distribution, utilities, or remote service operations.

SAP S/4HANA Sales is typically not designed as an offline-first platform. While it provides strong transactional and operational capabilities, its architecture and user experience are generally optimized for connected ERP-centric scenarios rather than disconnected mobile sales operations.

Evaluation Spreadsheet

The following table provides a consolidated comparison of the three primary Sales Order Management solution options discussed across this document and the two previous articles related to SAP Customer Experience solutions. It combines both functional and architectural considerations to help Enterprise Architects evaluate the strengths, limitations, and typical positioning of each option within different transformation scenarios.

The comparison includes capabilities and decision criteria associated with SAP S/4HANA Sales, SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, and SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C), providing a comprehensive overview of the most relevant aspects commonly evaluated during target architecture discussions and Sales transformation initiatives.

 

 Criteria

SAP S/4HANA Sales

SAP Sales Cloud V2

SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C)

Operational Integration

Typical Customer Scenarios

ERP-Centric Manufacturing Organizations

Organizations operating highly integrated manufacturing and supply chain environments often position SAP S/4HANA Sales as the primary platform for Sales Order Management due to tight integration requirements with pricing, ATP, logistics, fulfillment, and financial processes.

Customer Engagement Transformation Programs

Organizations prioritizing sales productivity, customer engagement, mobility, and omnichannel interactions may prefer SAP Sales Cloud solutions integrated with SAP S/4HANA fulfillment capabilities.

Simplification Initiatives

Some organizations intentionally consolidate sales processes into SAP S/4HANA to reduce application landscape complexity, simplify integrations, and centralize operational processes.

Field Sales and Offline Scenarios

Organizations with strong offline mobility requirements may continue leveraging SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C), particularly in scenarios where uninterrupted mobile access remains a critical business requirement.

Conclusion

Sales Order Management remains one of the most strategically important capabilities within the Lead-to-Cash process and continues to play a central role in SAP transformation programs.

As demonstrated throughout this document, SAP S/4HANA Sales remains a strong and valid option for organizations prioritizing operational integration, transactional consistency, advanced pricing, fulfillment orchestration, and simplified ERP-centric architectures. In many industries, especially those with highly operational or logistics-intensive business models, maintaining Sales Order Management directly within SAP S/4HANA can reduce architectural complexity while improving end-to-end process visibility and execution efficiency.

At the same time, organizations should carefully evaluate whether their transformation objectives extend beyond transactional execution into broader customer engagement and sales experience modernization initiatives. In these scenarios, SAP Sales Cloud solutions may provide additional capabilities related to mobility, guided selling, opportunity management, collaboration, AI-assisted sales execution, and omnichannel customer engagement.

Ultimately, the target architecture decision should not be driven solely by product preference, but rather by the organization’s business priorities, business capabilities, operational model, user expectations, integration strategy, and long-term enterprise architecture vision.

There is no universally correct approach for all organizations. The most appropriate solution depends on the balance between operational efficiency, customer engagement requirements, architectural simplification objectives, and transformation strategy.

entry.title: Contacting Your SAP Account Executive: A Guide!
entry.content:
Let us introduce the role of the Account Executive and the significant part they play. 

The Account Executive (AE) or Account Owner serves as the essential bridge between SAP and the organization that has entered a contract with SAP. The AE functions as the primary point of contact for the organization, ensuring seamless communication and coordination.  

They are responsible for managing all aspects related to support, product inquiries, retention strategies, and service-related matters. By fostering a strong relationship, the Account Executive ensures that the organization receives comprehensive and personalized assistance, thereby enhancing the overall customer experience. 

The following are examples of topics that can be addressed to your Account Executive:  
• Clarifications on contract verbiage 
• Updates to company address 
• Plans to upgrade software or hardware 
• Access to trial or demo software 
• Questions regarding Service Level Agreements (SLA) 
• Information on education and training events 
• Details on products included with licenses 
• Maintenance renewal inquiries 
• License compliance status 
• Utilization of licenses for compliance 
• Required licenses for the account 
• Information on new purchases/contracts 
• Software usage permissions based on the contract 
• Costs and fees associated with products or services 
• Details about specific license materials (what they are, what they do, etc.) 
• Cloud System/Tenant changes and issues (new cloud products not available yet, etc.) 
• Cloud Installations (deletion, cloud installations not available) 
• Issues with the company's account/contract preventing software downloads and/or generating permanent license keys as indicated by SAP Support 
• Confirmation from the Account Executive is required before submitting a request through CIC for deletion of all Super Administrators if there is no active maintenance linked to the user's account 

Steps to find your AE using the self-service option: 

  1. Navigate to the SAP for Me page (https://me.sap.com/) 
  1. Go to the Users and Contacts Dashboard 
  1. Select Overview  

Users & Contacts - SAP for Me 

For further guidance, please refer to the Knowledge Base Article on how to contact the Account Executive: SAP Note: 2626344 

If you are still unable to connect with the Account Executive, the Customer Interaction Center  is always available to assist you by providing the Account Executive's information from SAP to your organization.  

The Customer Interaction Center can provide the name, email address, and phone number of the Account Executive (AE) or account owner by requesting the Customer ERP number or installation number, the email address with the same domain ID, and the S-User ID. 

https://support.sap.com/en/contact-us.html?anchorId=section 

 Thanks for reading! 

 We hope you found this helpful. Please note that the Customer Interaction Center is always happy to guide you on any of the mentioned processes and assist you with any queries you may have! 

Read more Customer Interaction Center blog posts Here   

If you have any questions, please feel free to ask us in the Q&A All Questions in Customer Experience | SAP Community and All Questions in Support Services | SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Issue on using System User for Scheduled CBTA execution
entry.content:
The user has to be of type "Dialog" to be able to logon to the SUT. System user will not work.

Please see the documentation in the system SU01 - move to mouse to Field "User Type" and hit F1.

System 'B'

Use the system user type for internal system processes (-> background processing) or system-related processes (-> ALE, workflow, TMS, CUA).

Dialog logon (using SAP GUI) is not possible.

A user of this type is excluded from the general settings for password validity. Only user administrators can change the password using transaction SU01 (Goto -> Change Password).

entry.title: Re: EDI in the age of agentic AI, the structural mismatch nobody is naming
entry.content:
Thank you, Egbert.

On maturity, SAP Business Network is proven and widely adopted. Now 29+ years old (established in 1997), it is the largest and most mature network of its kind in the industry, with asset and logistics processes added more recently in 2016 and 2018. Thousands of customers, including almost all of the world’s largest companies, use it to collaborate with their supply chain in some form.

IDC recently (2024) conducted an analysis of both the buy and sell sides. The results were compelling:

  • Buy side: 404% ROI, 30% faster go-to-market, 27% faster product/order delivery time
  • Sell side: 416% ROI, 20% faster go-to-market, 50% faster time to invoice

As a smart follower, this is mature and well beyond early-adopter territory.

entry.title: Re: 在采购订单表头新增自定义字段失败
entry.content:
Good day @caoka 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the translation and the lack of exact screenshots that display the error you face, I can say the following.

Achieving that in Public Cloud will depend on the field being created under the correct business context and the required usages being enabled.

For your scenario, because the purchase order is created via interface and the coding/process value must be returned to SAP, you should check whether the custom field was enabled for the relevant purchase order UI/API usage. See KBA 3034002 for more details on enabling purchase order custom fields.

In simple terms:

  • Creating the custom field is not enough.
  • You must create is in the correct Purchases Order Header business context.
  • The required usage must be enabled, for example, UI, API, form template, or reporting.
  • If the purchase order is created or updated via interface, the interface must support the custom field.
  • If the custom field fails when added, check whether the field type, usage, or business context is supported for purchase order header.
  • Field control for purchase order custom fields is controlled through extensibility/BAdIs, not through classic SSCUI field selection. See KBA 3268822 for more details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, S
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, Scalably, and Cleanly

1.      Introduction

Utilities are accelerating their digital transformation, but one architectural frontier remains notoriously difficult to conquer:

How do you safely ingest high-frequency Operational Technology (OT) data from substations, feeders, and Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) into SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) without introducing cyber vulnerabilities or disrupting grid reliability?

Legacy architecture often relied on brittle, custom-coded point-to-point connections or heavy middleware that violated basic security isolation principles. This article provides a comprehensive, production-ready, architecture-driven blueprint for securely integrating:

  • IEC 61850 substation telemetry (GOOSE/MMS)
  • MQTT/Sparkplug B telemetry streams
  • SCADA / RTU / IED asynchronous events
  • Grid-edge IoT sensors

We will achieve this by leverage modern event-driven architectures (EDA), Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA), and SAP Clean Core design patterns.

2.      Why OT-to-IT Integration Challenges Enterprise

Bridging the physical grid and the digital cloud requires reconciling two fundamentally opposing engineering philosophies:

Dimension

Operational Technology (OT)

Enterprise Information Technology (IT)

Primary Priority

Availability & Safety: Human life and grid stability. Zero downtime tolerated.

Confidentiality & Integrity: Data security, compliance, and financial accuracy.

Protocols

Industrial, low-overhead, non-IP, or deterministic (IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus).

Web-native, human-readable, RESTful (HTTPS, OData, gRPC).

Network Philosophy

Air-gapped, strictly segmented (Purdue Model Level 0-3), highly localized.

Cloud-native, perimeterless, distributed, global access.

Data Characteristics

Ultra-high frequency (milliseconds), time-series bursts, massive velocity.

Transactional, structured, stateful, request-response.

To bridge this gap safely, cloud ingestion must be completely outbound-only from the lower levels, strictly audited, cryptographically verified, and decoupled via an enterprise event broker.

3.      Business impact on Utilities

This architecture is not just a technical modernization—it directly enables:

  • Reduced unplanned outages through real-time anomaly detection
  • Lower maintenance costs via predictive asset health insights
  • Improved regulatory compliance with secure audit-ready telemetry flows
  • Faster field response through automated S/4HANA work order generation
  • Scalable grid-edge onboarding without re-platforming legacy OT systems

4.      Reference Architecture: Purdue Model to SAP BTP

The architecture below maps the telemetry flow across the classic Purdue Model for Industrial Control Systems, ensuring that no direct connection is ever established between the cloud and Level 2/3 critical infrastructure.

5.      Architectural Safeguards Highlighted:

  • Directional Isolation: Firewalls block all inbound traffic to the Substation OT network. The Industrial Edge Gateway establishes an outbound connection to the Upper DMZ broker.
  • Decoupling Layer: SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) acts as the shock absorber, ensuring that high-frequency grid telemetry bursts do not overwhelm downstream ERP APIs.

*Architectural Principle: Direct OT-to-cloud integrations without DMZ brokers are inherently unsafe.  Any architecture that bypasses the industrial DMZ violates fundamental Purdue Model isolation and must be avoided.

6.      Deep-Dive: Protocol Translation & Topic Design

 The Conversion: IEC 61850 to Lightweight Cloud Events: IEC 61850 uses Manufacturing Message Specification (MMS) for complex reporting and Generic Object-Oriented Substation Events (GOOSE) for peer-to-peer microsecond trips. These cannot be parsed by cloud apps directly.

An Edge Gateway maps these complex hierarchical Logical Nodes (e.g., MMXU1 for Three-Phase Voltage/Current) into standard JSON structures wrapped in the CloudEvents specification.

Production-Grade Ingestion Payload Example

Below is the exact schema passed from the Upper DMZ to SAP Advanced Event Mesh when a transformer experiences a thermal anomaly:

JSON { "specversion": "1.0",

  "id": "evt-773a-4b92-88ef-22114949ad45", "source": "/us-east/substation-s12/transformer-tx03",   "type":

"com.utility.grid.telemetry.v1",

  "datacontenttype": "application/json",

  "time": "2026-06-01T18:21:55.003Z",

  "data": { "logical_node": "YPTR1",

    "component": "WindingTemperature",

    "metrics": {  "temperature_celsius": 98.4,

      "ambient_celsius": 32.1,

      "cooling_fan_status": "ON_MAX" },

    "status": { "health_score": 72.5,

      "anomaly_detected": true,

      "severity": "WARNING" } } }

Enterprise Topic Hierarchy Best Practices

To optimize routing efficiency within SAP Advanced Event Mesh and avoid bloated subscription wildcards, we enforce a structured, predictable topic hierarchy layout:

$$\text{Domain} \,/\, \text{Business Unit} \,/\, \text{Geographic Zone} \,/\, \text{Substation ID} \,/\, \text{Asset Category} \,/\, \text{Asset ID} \,/\, \text{Event Type}$$

Examples:

  • grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry
  • grid/transmission/midwest/sub-s45/circuit-breaker/cb07/state

This allows BTP consumers to subscribe with surgical precision. For instance, a predictive maintenance engine can listen to all transformer telemetry across the entire Northeast region using the pattern: grid/distribution/northeast/+/transformer/+/telemetry.

7.      The Blueprint in Action: A Practitioner Scenario

To understand how this architecture operates under pressure, let's trace a real-world grid event: A severe thermal anomaly on Substation Transformer TX03 located in the Northeast distribution zone.

Step 1: The Edge Trigger (Purdue Level 2)

  1. The physical temperature sensor on Transformer TX03 detects a sudden spike, hitting $98.4^\circ\text{C}$.
  2. The local Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) broadcasts this telemetry via an IEC 61850 MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification) reporting block over the substation's internal fiber optic bus.

Step 2: Protocol Translation & Hardening (Industrial DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The OT Industrial Edge Gateway (running Litmus Edge) intercepts the raw MMS packet.
  2. It strips out the heavy industrial headers, extracts the data fields from the Logical Node YPTR1 (the standard IEC 61850 node for a power transformer), and maps them into a standardized, web-friendly JSON payload.
  3. The gateway packages this payload inside a standardized CloudEvents v1.0 wrapper.

Step 3: Secure Egress to the DMZ Proxy (Enterprise DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The Edge Gateway initiates an outbound-only TLS 1.3 connection to the Upper DMZ MQTT Broker (HiveMQ Cluster).
  2. It passes hardware-secured mTLS client certificates to prove its identity and publishes the message to the strict, isolated topic:

 “grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry”

Step 4: Ingestion by SAP Advanced Event Mesh (SAP BTP Layer)

  1. The HiveMQ cluster securely routes the incoming stream over a secure WebSocket connection into SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via Dynamic Message Routing (DMR).
  2. Because AEM natively understands the hierarchical topic layout, it instantly clones and routes the message to three concurrent, decoupled subscribers without any database lag:
    • SAP Datasphere Ingestion Queue: For long-term historical time-series storage and grid analytics.
    • SAP AI Core Engine: To recalculate the transformer's Remaining Useful Life (RUL) score.
    • CAP Event Processor Queue: Dedicated to immediate, critical rule evaluation.

Step 5: Real-Time Orchestration & Clean Core Action

  1. A lightweight SAP CAP (Cloud Application Model) microservice consumes the message from its dedicated AEM queue.
  2. The service executes an automated rule: If temperature_celsius > $95^\circ\text{C}$ and cooling_fan_status == "ON_MAX", trigger emergency field intervention.
  3. Rather than writing complex custom ABAP tables inside the ERP, the CAP service uses the standard SAP Integration Suite to call the out-of-the-box S/4HANA OData v4 API (API_EQUIPMENT_SRV).
  4. The Result: An automated Urgent Corrective Maintenance Work Order is instantly generated in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management with a high-priority dispatch flag—all accomplished within 2.3 seconds of the physical temperature spike, leaving the digital core clean, secure, and upgrade-ready.

8.      Advanced Substation-to-BTP Integration Patterns

 *Reality Check*: Batch ingestion architectures are fundamentally incompatible with modern grid telemetry. Millisecond-level data streams require real-time, event-driven processing models—not delayed batch pipelines.

Depending on your edge compute availability and governance requirements, choose one of these production deployment topologies:

Pattern A: Multi-Tiered Message Broker Architecture (Recommended for Zero-Trust)

  • Mechanism: An Edge Gateway pushes local data to an on-premises DMZ MQTT cluster (e.g., Hive MQ). Secure bridge configuration clones and forwards permitted topics out to SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via cloud connectors or secure WebSocket’s.
  • Pros: Strict isolation; if the cloud connection drops, the local DMZ broker caches events, mitigating data loss.

Pattern B: Intelligent Edge Compute with Stream Processing (Recommended for High Frequency)

  • Mechanism: Deploy an industrial edge compute node (running lightweight runtimes like Node-RED or KubeEdge) physically inside the substation. It aggregates millisecond telemetry, calculates rolling 1-minute means/standard deviations, and publishes only summarized statistical health events to SAP BTP.
  • Pros: Radically reduces egress data costs and avoids overwhelming BTP with trivial data points.

9.      Security & Governance Blueprint (Non-Negotiable Controls)

Bringing OT data to the cloud is impossible without an airtight security architecture. Implement the following parameters at each layer:

  • Mutual TLS (mTLS) with Hardware Roots of Trust: Every edge gateway must authenticate against the DMZ broker using X.509 client certificates issued by an internal Utility Certificate Authority (CA). Private keys must be stored in secure TPM 2.0 hardware chips on the edge device.
  • Granular Access Control Lists (ACLs): Edge gateways must be explicitly barred from publishing to topics outside their assigned asset scope. A compromise of Substation A must never allow injection of malicious events into Substation B.
  • Schema Validation at the Ingress Gate: Use the Schema Validation features in SAP Advanced Event Mesh. If a message arrives containing bad structures, malformed types, or executable code payloads, it is immediately shunted to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for forensics, blocking code injection vectors.
  • Network Partitioning & Backpressure Controls: If the connection between the utility DMZ and SAP BTP degrades, the DMZ broker must utilize Local Storage Buffering. High QoS (Quality of Service) 1 or 2 settings must be applied strictly to critical operational state changes (e.g., breaker trips) to guarantee delivery, while standard continuous metrics use QoS 0 to save bandwidth.

10. Realizing Enterprise Value in SAP BTP

Once normalized grid events stream cleanly into SAP Advanced Event Mesh, they become actionable assets without touching the digital core directly:

*Strategic Insight*: Event Mesh is not optional—it is the control plane for grid-scale ingestion.  Without event-driven decoupling, high-frequency OT telemetry cannot be safely or scalable integrated into enterprise systems.

The Event Consumer Microservices Layer

Deploy lightweight, stateless microservices within the SAP BTP Kyma Runtime or SAP Cloud Application Model (CAP) to process incoming streams:

 

 

 

  • Real-Time Grid Insights: Kyma functions scan the event properties. If a transformer temperature exceeds defined thresholds (temperature_celsius > 95), an immediate event is triggered.
  • AI-Driven Asset Health Optimization: Data streams scale out directly into SAP Datasphere and SAP AI Core. Machine Learning models calculate remaining useful life (RUL) metrics based on historical load/thermal characteristics, sending updated health indices back to operational dashboards.

The Clean Core Imperative

By processing events asynchronously in BTP, the core enterprise system (SAP S/4HANA / IS-U) remains entirely untouched by heavy custom code. S/4HANA only participates when action is required—such as creating an asset maintenance order or executing a field service dispatch via standard standard OData v4 APIs. The system updates and upgrades effortlessly, unencumbered by massive custom databases full of RAW time-series data.

11. End-to-End Flow: Transformer Overheating Scenario

  • A transformer in Substation S12 exceeds thermal threshold (98.4°C).
  • IEC 61850 MMS reports are captured by Edge Gateway.
  • Gateway converts payload to Cloud Events JSON.
  • Event is securely published (mTLS) to DMZ MQTT broker.
  • Broker forwards allowed topics to SAP Advanced Event Mesh.
  • AEM routes the event to a CAP microservice.
  • CAP evaluates rules → triggers anomaly event.
  • SAP AI Core enriches with predictive health score.
  • SAP S/4HANA automatically creates a maintenance work order.
  • Field service team is dispatched proactively.

12. Operational Summary Blueprint

  1. The convergence of OT and IT is no longer optional—it is foundational to modern utilities.
  2. However, success depends not on connectivity alone, but on *how* that connectivity is governed. 
  3. Architectures that prioritize strict isolation, event-driven decoupling, and Clean Core principles will define the next generation of grid intelligence.
  4. SAP BTP, when used correctly, becomes not just an integration platform—but the control tower for real-time, autonomous utility operations.

entry.title: Re: Additional “Attributes” button is displayed in CJ8VH...
entry.content:
Hello, 

The additional "Attributes" button appearing in the post-upgrade environment is a standard SAP variant management feature. It allows users to maintain variant properties such as user-specific settings, default flags, and field visibility.

This is a UI enhancement delivered in the upgraded system version and has no functional impact on existing variants or processes. May I know if you face any error or difficulties?

entry.content: Great Insights

entry.title: Re: I want to modify the warehouse number that has alread...
entry.content:
Hello @SAPB1JIMI 


You cannot change an existing Warehouse Number once it is used in SAP.

try,
Create a new Warehouse Number
Reconfigure assignments
Transfer/migrate data (if needed)
Deactivate old warehouse (no deletion if used)

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: altSuperID is null
entry.content:
In SuccessFactors Learning ODATA API I am trying to obtain alternate supervisor IDs for a given user.

Sadly, despite the supervisors being set in the LMS, the altSuperID1, 2 and 3 fields are null instead of holding the userIDs.

SAP Support replied with a KB Article which says it's expected, because a user could have even up to 200 alternate managers and that the only way out is an enhancement request.

I wanted to ask if anyone has hit this obstacle in alt super IDs and worked around it?

--- Steps to Reproduce ---

  1. Go to LMS, search for a user, add an alternate manager to that user.
  2. Obtain LMS Token for Admin
  3. Call /learning/odatav4/user/v1/Users('userID')
  4. Observe all altSuperID are null. At least one of them should be populated as it is in the LMS.

entry.title: Automatic Account Determination in Actual Costing: Transaction Keys and General Modifications
entry.content:
This blog tries to answer the following questions in Actual Costing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • When CKMLCP posts closing journal entries, which G/L accounts are used and how are they determined?
  • What is the difference between selecting and unselecting "Revaluate Material" in the Post Closing step?
  • What are the PRY (and KDM) general modifications (PNL, PPL, PSL, PWP, empty), and what business process does each one represent?
    • Example: SG124_MA06 and FG126_MA06
  • How to configure Automatic Account Determination (SSCUI 100297) to post price differences from different processes to different G/L accounts?

Introduction

In my introductory blog on Actual Costing, I walked through a complete end-to-end example to show how price variances from raw material procurement and production are collected, rolled up, and allocated to COGS and ending inventory at period end. The Post Closing step in CKMLCP posts journal entries using transaction keys such as PRY, KDM, and BSX.

This blog goes deeper into those transaction keys, i.e. what each one means, how the "Revaluate Material" checkbox changes the posting behavior, and most importantly, how the General Modification feature of PRY and KDM lets you differentiate postings by process type. This is a powerful tool for you if you need granular P&L reporting on where price differences end up.

Transaction Keys Used in Actual Costing Closing Entries

When CKMLCP (Edit Actual Costing Runs) runs the Post Closing step, it posts journal entries using these transaction keys. You assign G/L accounts to them in SSCUI 100297 (Automatic Account Determination). The bold ones are specific to Actual Costing.

Transaction Key

What It Posts

BSX

Inventory account revaluation (stock at actual cost)

PRY

Price differences (the key Actual Costing transaction key)

KDM

Exchange rate differences on inventory

PRL

Activity price differences (from cost center to material)

GBB-AUI

Activity price difference credited to cost center

WPM

Price difference of material WIP build-up

PRM

Price difference of material WIP reduction

WPA

Price difference of activity WIP build-up

PRA

Price difference of activity WIP reduction

LKW

Accruals and deferrals (statistical, when not revaluating inventory)

COC

Revaluation of other consumption (when movement type group not in ML config)

UMB

Revenue/expense from revaluation (e.g., MR22, CKMLCP Post Closing)

In the introductory blog example, you could see PRY, KDM, BSX, and GBB all appearing in the same journal entry 4700000297. This blog explains how each of those postings is determined.

The "Revaluate Material" Checkbox — BSX vs. LKW

When you run the Post Closing step in CKMLCP, one of the key parameters is "Revaluate Material".

When selected (default behavior):
The system posts a real inventory revaluation. The inventory account (BSX) is debited. The price difference account (PRY) is credited.

  • Debit   BSX – Inventory account          +160.00 EUR  (stock revalued to actual cost)
  • Credit  PRY – Price differences          -160.00 EUR

When unselected:
The system uses a statistical posting instead. The inventory balance stays at standard price.

  • Debit   LKW – Accruals/Deferrals         +160.00 EUR   (statistical; no inventory impact)
  • Credit  PRY – Price differences          -160.00 EUR

The LKW option is used by customers who do not want to touch the inventory balance sheet account but still want price differences visible in P&L for analytical purposes.

General Modifications for PRY and KDM — Differentiate by Process

By default, all price differences land on the single G/L account assigned to PRY. Many customers want more analytical details:

  • How much of our price differences stayed in inventory vs. flowed to downstream products?
  • How much relates to WIP that we couldn't yet settle?
  • Which P&L account should capture differences from direct cost center consumption?

This is exactly what General Modification solves. By enabling it in SSCUI 100297, you can route each posting type to a different G/L account.

The Five General Modifications

General Modification

Label

Meaning

Posted on

PNL

Next Level

Price diff transferred to a higher-level material via multi-level rollup

The input (lower-level) material

PPL

Previous Level

Price difference received from a lower-level material

The output (higher-level) material

PSL

Single Level

Price diff from single-level consumption revaluation (cost centers, sales orders)

The consumed material

PWP

WIP

Price diff in WIP revaluation (open production orders at period end)

The material with open WIP

(empty)

Ending Inventory

Price diff remaining in ending inventory

The same material

Key relationship between PNL and PPL: They are always mirror entries for the same amount: one material "sends" via PNL, the other "receives" via PPL. Across all materials in scope, PNL and PPL net to zero.

The same five sub-modifications apply identically to KDM: KDM-PNL, KDM-PPL, KDM-PSL, KDM-PWP, KDM-empty.

You can refer to below configuration screenshots in SSCUI 100297, which is also used for our example in next section:

  • PRY with general modifications per valuation class:

Figure 1: PRY Configuration

 

  • KDM with general modifications shared by all valuation classes:

Figure 2: KDM Configuration

Example: SG124_MA06 and FG126_MA06

The following example uses data from SAP internal test system. SG124_MA06 is a semi-finished material (Plant 1010, Period 2/2026) consumed as input to produce three different finished goods:

Input Material

 

Output Material

Qty Consumed

SG124_MA06 (Semi-finished)

→

FG126_MA06

772 PC

SG124_MA06 (Semi-finished)

→

FG228_BB6_MA06

10 PC

SG124_MA06 (Semi-finished)

→

FG0612_MA06-1

10 PC

The following picture from Material Price Analysis (CKM3N) shows above consumption clearly:

Figure 3: SG124_MA06 Aggregated Consumption

 SG124_MA06 — Where Differences Originate

During the period, SG124_MA06 cumulates an inventory of 1485 PC with total price differences of 135.93 EUR (PRY) and exchange rate differences of 60.07 EUR (KDM).

After expanding folder of each produced product in CKM3N (Material Price Analysis) as above, under the Consumption section:

  • The cumulative quantity of 792 PC is broken down into individual finished goods
  • The PriceDiff column shows how the total price difference of 135.93 EUR (PRY) is distributed into finished goods (2.57, 70.70, 2.56 EUR respectively — all via PRY-PNL, G/L 52041500).
  • The ExRateDiff column shows how the total exchange rate difference of 60.07 EUR (KDM) is distributed into finished goods (0.40, 31.23, 0.41 EUR respectively — all via KDM-PNL, G/L 72020000)
  • The WIP row shows 1.73 EUR via PRY-PWP, and ending inventory shows 58.37 EUR via PRY-empty. 

Figure 4: SG124_MA06 Consumption Expanded

 

Destination

Quantity

PRY Share (EUR)

KDM Share (EUR)

Modification

(empty)

Total cumulative

1485 PC

60.07

 

How This Looks in CKM3 — FG126_MA06

Figure 5: FG126_MA06

In CKM3N (Material Price Analysis) for FG126_MA06, the 70.70 EUR (PRY-PPL) and 31.23 EUR (KDM-PPL) from SG124_MA06 appear under Receipts → Production → SG124_MA06/1010. The 70.70 EUR on SG124_MA06 (PNL → G/L 52041500) and the 70.70 EUR on FG126_MA06 (PPL → G/L 52042000) are mirror entries for the same amount.

FI Journal Entry — Post Closing for SG124_MA06

The journal entry posted by CKMLCP (document 4700000297, ML Post Closing, 28.02.2026) contains the following key lines. Each uses a different G/L account from SSCUI 100297:

Figure 6: Closing Journal Entries from CKMLCP

 Journal Entry 4700000297: ML Post Closing — SG124_MA06 — Period 2/2026

Ln

G/L Account

Account Name

Key

Mod.

WIP exch.rate diff

The posting direction follows a consistent pattern: on the sending material (SG124_MA06), PRY-PNL is a Credit — the price difference

The posting direction follows a consistent pattern: on the sending material (SG124_MA06), PRY-PNL is a Credit — the price difference is "leaving" via multilevel rollup. On the receiving finished goods, PRY-PPL is a Debit — the same amount is "arriving". WIP (PWP) and ending inventory (empty) are also Credits on SG124, representing differences that stay rather than flow onwards.

How to Configure General Modifications in SSCUI 100297

  1. Open SSCUI 100297 — Automatic Account Determination
  2. Select area Sourcing and Procurement
  3. Specify transaction key PRY (or KDM), company code / chart of accounts
  4. In the Account Assignments section, click Edit Rules
  5. Enable the checkbox General Modification (optionally also Valuation Class for per-material-type differentiation)
  6. Add one row per sub-modification and assign your desired G/L account numbers

As shown in the SSCUI screenshots in the beginning, the example configuration has PRY with both General Modification and Valuation Class (3000 Raw Materials, 7900 Semifinished, 7920 Finished Goods), while KDM has no valuation class differentiation. 

Transaction Key

General Modification

Valuation Class

G/L Account

PRY

(empty)

3000 / 7900 / 7920

52043000

PRY

PNL

3000 / 7900 / 7920

52041500

PRY

PPL

3000 / 7900 / 7920

52042000

PRY

PSL

3000 / 7900 / 7920

52045000

PRY

PWP

3000 / 7900 / 7920

52046000

KDM

(empty)

—

72010000

KDM

PNL

—

72020000

KDM

PPL

—

72040000

KDM

PSL

—

72050000

KDM

PWP

—

72050100

Tip: If you don't need per-process differentiation, simply assign one account to PRY and one to KDM without any General Modification; the system uses them as a catch-all for all sub-modification types.

Why Does This Matter for Margin Analysis?

  1. Multi-level cost flow transparency: Of the total 135.93 EUR in SG124_MA06, exactly 75.83 EUR flowed to the three finished goods via account 52041500 (PNL). G/L 52042000 (PPL) on the receiving materials shows the same gross amount — enabling BOM cost tracing in your trial balance.
  2. WIP monitoring: Account 52046000 (PWP) isolates the 1.73 EUR locked in open production order 1012505 at period end. It will only be released when the order settles next period.
  3. PNL/PPL netting: The 70.70 EUR on SG124_MA06 (PNL, 52041500) and on FG126_MA06 (PPL, 52042000) net to zero across both materials — but on separate accounts, enabling gross BOM cost flow reporting.
  4. CO-PA integration: With General Modification, PSL on COGS uses G/L 52045000, enabling PA reports to distinguish "direct consumption variance" from "multilevel rollup variance" at product/customer level.

Summary

Topic

Key Points

Inventory revaluation account

BSX (real) or LKW (statistical): controlled by "Revaluate Material" checkbox

Price / exchange rate difference accounts

PRY for price diffs, KDM for exchange rate difference

Available general modifications

PNL (next level), PPL (from lower), PSL (single-level), PWP (WIP), empty (ending inventory)

PNL + PPL net to zero

Yes — always mirror entries for the same amount across the two materials

Where to configure

SSCUI 100297 → Edit Rules → Enable General Modification

Further differentiate by material type

Yes — additionally enable Valuation Class

General Modification mandatory

No — optional; without it all PRY/KDM use one catch-all account

Related Resources

  • Actual Costing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — introductory blog
  • WIP Revaluation in SAP Actual Costing
  • Joint Production in Product Costing in SAP Cloud ERP
  • SAP Note 3617837: FAQ of Actual Costing in S/4HANA Cloud (Q14 covers account determination)

entry.title: Re: SAP Convergent Charging 2022.2 updater becomes slow/u...
entry.content:
Hi Marius,

Thanks for contacting us.
The documents we normally require are listed in KBA 2667659 ("Data to retrieve for a first-level analysis of a SAP CC platform"). This includes the LOG and TRC files and, more importantly, the Garbage Collection log, the status of the cache (where the created subscriber data is held), and the thread dump files you mentioned (this is what we mostly need when troubleshooting performance).
Usually, it's not necessary to generate other reports directly from the JVM itself nor from the OS.
Since this is a considerable amount of sensitive data, it's best to file an actual customer ticket (you can reference this conversation there, for follow-up).

Based on what the documents show, we may have to ask you to adjust the trace settings (to get more thread dumps, or more traces from CC).
For now, as suggested by your message, there are numerous details to consider, so it's a bit difficult to address everything at once, especially before getting the usual info.
In the meantime, we can at least confirm that the way you use the updaters (active-passive mode, without any load balancer in the middle) is the proper way to go, for SAP CC.
Also, the audit feature alone is not expected to alter the performance, but it does rely on the database a lot. If the database is the bottleneck, the thread dumps should show it.

Another important point is the recommend initial tuning: can you please make sure that your CC system already complies with KBA 2006073 ("JVM option recommendations, up to SAP CC 2022")?
This KBA is supposed to be sufficient for productive systems, but different settings can be considered based on the context (for example if you temporarily need to improve the updater's performance during a heavy provisioning cycle).

Best regards.

François
SAP Convergent Charging Support

entry.title: Re: SAC Comment widget date filter
entry.content:
Hi Samuel,

The only solution I’ve found is as follows:

  1. Set up a page filter using the custom date from your story.
  2. Link the page filter to the comment widget only.

The comment widget should then display only the comments for the selected date whenever you change the story filter.

Best regards,

Matthias

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 05 - Portable functions
OData vs SOAP for .NET WMS Integration in SAP Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello Community,

I am seeking technical advice regarding the integration of a .NET-based WMS solution developed by our team with  SAP Business ByDesign.

As we move away from historical on-premise setups toward a decoupled 'Side-by-Side' architecture, we want to use the app with SAP Public Cloud . I have two critical questions for those who have managed similar cloud transitions:

1. API Utilization for .NET Integration
Since our WMS middleware is built on .NET, we need to ensure robust communication with the SAP core.

  • Which specific OData services or SOAP APIs are recommended for real-time warehouse tasks (e.g., Goods Receipt, Stock Transfer, Picking)in SAP Public Cloud ?
  • For those using SAP Public Cloud, are you primarily using the SiteLogisticsTask or WarehouseConfirmation web services, and how do they perform under the high-frequency request load typical of a .NET scanning application?

2. Public Cloud WM Capability & Responsiveness
We are evaluating how the Warehouse Management (WM) module in the Public Cloud responds to external 'exploits' or calls from a third-party .NET interface.

  • Does the Public Cloud WM module offer the same level of flexibility as the historical on-premise WM/EWM when it comes to API-triggered movements?
  • Are there known performance limitations or 'throttling' issues when a .NET application performs high-volume scanning directly against the Public Cloud tenant?
  • In your experience, does the Public Cloud WM architecture provide sufficient coverage for complex scenarios when managed via external APIs?

We want to ensure that our project respects the 'Clean Core' strategy while maintaining the historical reliability that  our company has built into the  App over the years.

I would greatly appreciate any technical documentation, API maps, or feedback from your own cloud WMS implementations.

Best regards,

entry.title: Re: Restriction to avoid printing parked receipts, even m...
entry.content:
Hi Jhon,

You can exclude printing parked receipts in the print template configuration.
Each template has additional options. For the sales receipt template, there is an option called "Print parked receipts." Once you add this option and set it to false, printing of parked receipts will be disabled.

Best regards,
Martin

entry.title: Re: How to Prevent Duplicate GST Numbers in Customer and...
entry.content:
Hi @SivaNagiReddy ,

Yes, if you maintain the GST Number in the Identification tab using the tax number category IN3 (GST Registration Number), SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides a standard option to perform duplicate GST number validation.

Please refer to the following KBAs for detailed information:

KBA 2886187 – How to Activate Tax Number Category Duplication Check for Business Partners in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

KBA 2886241 – How to Change Duplicate Tax Number Category from an Information Message to an Error Message in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

You can use the following SSCUIs:

  • SSCUI 102415 – Maintain Tax Number Categories: To activate duplicate checks for GST numbers.

  • SSCUI 103601 – Change Message Control for Duplicate Tax Numbers: To change the duplicate tax number message from a warning/information message to an error message.

These configurations will help prevent the creation of Business Partners with duplicate GST numbers.

Regards,
Vinith

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 06 - Project specific configuration
Re: MR21 – New Price Field Greyed Out – Standard Price No...
entry.content:
The MR21 transaction for updating the new standard price is greyed out in a specific plant.

Check Material Ledger Activation

  • Confirm whether the Material Ledger (ML) is active in the plant.
  • Execute SPRO → CKMSTART to activate the Material Ledger if required.

Review Error Messages

  • Check for any system-generated error messages (e.g., CKPRCH 038 indicating missing authorization).

Else provide some information on those materials (type of material, etc.)
